chapter: Chapter 1 — Your Driver's Licence
title: GDL Path
keyPoint: Class 7 is the first GDL step: learner, newly licensed, then full licence.
summary: Nova Scotia uses Graduated Driver Licensing so new drivers build skill in stages. Restrictions are removed only after you pass the required tests and gain experience.
handbookSection: Chapter 1 — Your Driver's Licence, The Nova Scotia Licensing System

chapter: Chapter 1 — Your Driver's Licence
title: Zero Alcohol
keyPoint: Learners must keep a zero blood alcohol level every time they drive.
summary: A Class 7 learner has a stricter standard than the Criminal Code limit. Any alcohol in the body can put your learner licence at risk.
handbookSection: Chapter 1 — Your Driver's Licence, Learner's Licence Restrictions

chapter: Driver's Handbook — Updates
title: Transit Bus Yield
keyPoint: Yield to a stopped transit bus signalling back into traffic where the limit is 60 km/h or less.
summary: When approaching from behind, slow and let the bus re-enter if it is safe. This update applies to transit buses, not school buses.
handbookSection: Introduction — Driver's Handbook Updates, Yield to Transit Buses

chapter: Official Class 7 Knowledge Test
title: Two Test Parts
keyPoint: You must pass rules of the road and road sign recognition as separate parts.
summary: The Class 7 knowledge test checks traffic laws, safe driving, and road signs. A miss on one part means you only retake the part you did not pass.
handbookSection: Official Class 7 Knowledge Test — What You're Tested On

chapter: Chapter 1 — Your Driver's Licence
title: Class 7 Eligibility
keyPoint: You can apply at 16; age 16 or 17 needs parent or guardian consent.
summary: Class 7 is for Nova Scotia residents who meet the learner requirements. Bring required identification and complete the Registry steps before the licence is issued.
handbookSection: Chapter 1 — Your Driver's Licence, Getting a Learner's Licence

chapter: Chapter 1 — Your Driver's Licence
title: Pass Mark
keyPoint: Each test part has 20 questions; you need at least 16 correct on each.
summary: The learner knowledge test has a rules test and a signs test. Study both because passing one part does not cover a fail on the other.
handbookSection: Chapter 1 — Your Driver's Licence, How to Apply for a Learner's Licence

chapter: Chapter 1 — Your Driver's Licence
title: Learner Supervisor
keyPoint: Drive only with an experienced licensed driver in the front seat.
summary: A learner cannot drive alone. The supervising driver must be ready to help, and learner passenger limits still apply.
handbookSection: Chapter 1 — Your Driver's Licence, Learner's Licence Restrictions

chapter: Chapter 1 — Your Driver's Licence
title: Demerit Points
keyPoint: Demerit points hit learners and newly licensed drivers sooner than experienced drivers.
summary: Convictions under the Motor Vehicle Act can add points to your record. Serious or repeated offences can delay progress through GDL.
handbookSection: Chapter 1 — Your Driver's Licence, Demerit Points

chapter: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road
title: Traffic Lights
keyPoint: Green means go only when safe; yellow means stop if safe; red means stop.
summary: Do not enter an intersection just because the light is green. A flashing red works like a stop sign, and a flashing yellow means slow and proceed carefully.
imageAlt: Yellow traffic-signal-lights-ahead warning sign
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Traffic Signal Lights

chapter: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road
title: Crosswalk Priority
keyPoint: Always yield to pedestrians in marked or unmarked crosswalks.
summary: Intersections include crosswalks even when the paint is not visible. If a signal changes while someone is crossing, let them finish safely.
imageAlt: Pedestrian crosswalk sign with a walking person symbol
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Pedestrians and Crosswalks

chapter: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road
title: Intersection Yield
keyPoint: First in the intersection goes first; when arriving together, yield to the right.
summary: Right-of-way rules prevent guessing at intersections. Yield signs and stop signs can change the order, so obey the posted control first.
imageAlt: Red and white triangular yield sign
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Right-of-Way at Intersections

chapter: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road
title: Emergency Vehicles
keyPoint: Move right and stop for lights and sirens until the emergency vehicle passes.
summary: Leave the route clear and do not follow a responding fire vehicle within 150 metres. Also keep clear of stopped emergency vehicles assisting at collisions.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Emergency Vehicles

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Stop Signs
keyPoint: Stop completely, yield, then move only when the way is clear.
summary: A stop sign requires a full stop at the stop line, crosswalk, or edge of the road. Rolling through is not a stop.
imageAlt: Red octagonal stop sign with white STOP lettering
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Regulatory Signs

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Warning Signs
keyPoint: Yellow warning signs tell you to adjust before a hazard or road change.
summary: Warning signs can show curves, merging traffic, narrow roads, crossings, or slippery surfaces. Slow early and choose a safe lane position.
imageAlt: Yellow merge warning sign
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Warning Signs

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Double Solid Lines
keyPoint: Do not cross continuous double solid yellow lines to pass.
summary: Yellow lines separate traffic moving in opposite directions. Broken lines may permit passing when safe, but double solid yellow lines mean no passing either way.
imageAlt: Pavement marking diagram with double solid yellow lines
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Pavement Markings

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Work Zones
keyPoint: Orange signs mean temporary conditions: slow down, watch workers, and expect lane shifts.
summary: Work zones can change traffic flow quickly. Leave extra space, obey traffic control people, and follow temporary signs even if they differ from the usual road setup.
imageAlt: Orange human-activity sign showing workers active
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Construction / Work Zone Signs

chapter: Chapter 4 — Safety
title: Vehicle Check
keyPoint: Check brakes, tires, lights, mirrors, horn, and wipers before problems find you.
summary: A road-ready vehicle gives you time to react. Regular maintenance also reduces preventable breakdowns and failed inspections.
handbookSection: Chapter 4 — Safety, Vehicle Maintenance

chapter: Chapter 4 — Safety
title: Seat Belts
keyPoint: Seat belts keep occupants in place; airbags do not replace them.
summary: Belts and child restraints reduce injury in a sudden stop or crash. Everyone should be properly restrained before the vehicle moves.
handbookSection: Chapter 4 — Safety, Passenger Safety

chapter: Chapter 4 — Safety
title: Blind Spots
keyPoint: Mirrors help, but shoulder checks confirm the space beside you is clear.
summary: Set your mirrors before moving and scan them often. Always shoulder check before changing lanes, turning, or pulling away from the curb.
handbookSection: Chapter 4 — Safety, Defensive Driving and Blind Spots

chapter: Chapter 4 — Safety
title: ABS Braking
keyPoint: With ABS, press the brake firmly and steadily; do not pump it.
summary: Anti-lock brakes help you keep steering control during hard braking. If the vehicle does not have ABS, use controlled braking to avoid locking the wheels.
handbookSection: Chapter 4 — Safety, Emergency Braking

chapter: Chapter 5 — Adverse Driving Conditions
title: Night Driving
keyPoint: Slow enough that you can stop within the distance your headlights show.
summary: Night driving reduces visibility and reaction time. Dim high beams for oncoming vehicles and when following another vehicle closely.
handbookSection: Chapter 5 — Adverse Driving Conditions, Nighttime Driving

chapter: Chapter 5 — Adverse Driving Conditions
title: Bad Weather
keyPoint: Poor visibility or traction means slow down and increase following distance.
summary: Rain, snow, fog, and spray can hide hazards and lengthen stopping distance. Use low-beam headlights when visibility is reduced.
imageAlt: Yellow slippery-when-wet warning sign
handbookSection: Chapter 5 — Adverse Driving Conditions, Rain, Snow, and Fog

chapter: Chapter 5 — Adverse Driving Conditions
title: Hydroplaning
keyPoint: If tires ride on water, ease off the accelerator and steer straight.
summary: Hydroplaning removes tire contact with the road. Bridges, overpasses, and shaded areas can also freeze first, so treat them cautiously in cold weather.
handbookSection: Chapter 5 — Adverse Driving Conditions, Rain

chapter: Chapter 5 — Adverse Driving Conditions
title: Brake Failure
keyPoint: Pump the brake pedal; if needed, apply the parking brake gently.
summary: Try to rebuild pressure by pumping the pedal several times. Hold the parking brake release while applying it gently so the wheels do not lock.
handbookSection: Chapter 5 — Adverse Driving Conditions, Brake Failure

chapter: Chapter 6 — Driving and Impairment
title: First Drink Counts
keyPoint: Alcohol impairment starts with the first drink, not at a magic number.
summary: Drivers can be unsafe before reaching a criminal BAC threshold. Learners and other GDL drivers have a zero-alcohol requirement.
handbookSection: Chapter 6 — Driving and Impairment, Drinking and Driving

chapter: Chapter 6 — Driving and Impairment
title: Medication Warning
keyPoint: Prescription, non-prescription, herbal, and illegal drugs can all impair driving.
summary: Read labels and ask a pharmacist if a medicine may cause drowsiness, slow reactions, or poor judgment. Never mix impairing substances with driving.
handbookSection: Chapter 6 — Driving and Impairment, Drug Impairment

chapter: Chapter 6 — Driving and Impairment
title: Warn Range
keyPoint: A roadside result from 0.05 to 0.08 can still lead to a suspension.
summary: You do not need to be at 0.08 BAC to face consequences. For learners, the practical target is simple: zero alcohol.
handbookSection: Chapter 6 — Driving and Impairment, Drinking and Driving

chapter: Chapter 6 — Driving and Impairment
title: Sober Ride Plan
keyPoint: Plan the ride home before drinking or using any impairing substance.
summary: Judgment drops as impairment rises, so do not wait until later to decide. Use a sober driver, taxi, transit, or stay overnight.
handbookSection: Chapter 6 — Driving and Impairment, Being a Responsible Host or Hostess

chapter: Chapter 7 — Vehicle Registration and Insurance
title: Road-Ready Documents
keyPoint: A vehicle needs registration, plates, inspection, and insurance before highway use.
summary: Keep required proof available when driving. The vehicle must be legal to operate, not just mechanically able to move.
handbookSection: Chapter 7 — Vehicle Registration and Insurance, Registering a Vehicle

chapter: Chapter 7 — Vehicle Registration and Insurance
title: Registration Certificate
keyPoint: The certificate identifies the owner, vehicle, VIN, and registration details.
summary: Registration proves the vehicle is recorded with the province. Update ownership or vehicle information when required instead of relying on outdated papers.
handbookSection: Chapter 7 — Vehicle Registration and Insurance, Certificate of Registration

chapter: Chapter 7 — Vehicle Registration and Insurance
title: Safety Inspection
keyPoint: Registered vehicles and trailers need a valid safety inspection.
summary: Official inspection stations check key safety equipment. If a vehicle fails, defects must be repaired and the vehicle reinspected within the allowed period.
handbookSection: Chapter 7 — Vehicle Registration and Insurance, Safety Inspection

chapter: Chapter 7 — Vehicle Registration and Insurance
title: Liability Insurance
keyPoint: Carry proof of financial responsibility; the handbook lists $500,000 minimum liability.
summary: Drivers must have accepted insurance or proof of financial responsibility. Insurance is a legal requirement, not an optional add-on.
handbookSection: Chapter 7 — Vehicle Registration and Insurance, Insurance

chapter: Chapter 8 — Motorcycles
title: Motorcycle Licence
keyPoint: Motorcycle riders need a motorcycle licence or endorsement.
summary: A regular passenger-vehicle licence alone is not enough to ride a motorcycle. New motorcycle riders complete motorcycle-specific tests and GDL steps.
handbookSection: Chapter 8 — Motorcycles, Getting a Motorcycle Licence

chapter: Chapter 8 — Motorcycles
title: Motorcycle Learner Limits
keyPoint: No passengers, zero alcohol, and daylight-only riding for motorcycle learners.
summary: A motorcycle learner may ride only from one half-hour before sunrise to one half-hour after sunset. These limits reduce risk while skills are new.
handbookSection: Chapter 8 — Motorcycles, Learner Motorcycle Restrictions

chapter: Chapter 8 — Motorcycles
title: Motorcycle Lane Space
keyPoint: A motorcycle is entitled to the full use of a traffic lane.
summary: Do not crowd motorcycles or share their lane. Riders should stay visible and avoid lingering in other drivers' blind spots.
handbookSection: Chapter 8 — Motorcycles, Lane Position and Visibility

chapter: Chapter 8 — Motorcycles
title: Helmet Rule
keyPoint: Motorcycle riders and passengers must wear approved, fastened helmets.
summary: Eye protection and protective clothing also reduce injury and improve control. Gear matters because riders have far less crash protection than car occupants.
handbookSection: Chapter 8 — Motorcycles, Protective Clothing

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Do Not Enter
keyPoint: Do not enter that road, ramp, or lane from your direction.
summary: This regulatory sign protects against wrong-way movement. Stop and choose another legal route instead of continuing past the sign.
imageAlt: Red do-not-enter sign with a white horizontal bar
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Regulatory Signs

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: School Zones
keyPoint: When children are present: 30 km/h in 50 zones, 50 km/h where the approach limit is higher.
summary: School and playground signs mean children may move unpredictably. Slow early, scan both sides of the road, and be ready to stop.
imageAlt: Fluorescent yellow-green school zone sign
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, School and Playground Signs

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Flashing Arrow Boards
keyPoint: Arrow boards warn of lane closures or obstructions; pass on the indicated side.
summary: A two-arrow board means either side is safe to pass. A straight bar is caution mode and does not tell you that passing is safe.
imageAlt: Flashing arrow board with two arrow heads
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Flashing Light Units

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Railway Crossings
keyPoint: Slow, look, listen, and be ready to stop at railway crossings.
summary: A crossbuck marks the crossing itself, and an advance sign warns one is ahead. Stop when lights flash, a gate is down, or a train is approaching.
imageAlt: White railway crossbuck sign with red border
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Railway Signs

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Information Signs
keyPoint: Route and direction signs help you choose lanes and exits early.
summary: Green, blue, and white signs can show highway numbers, destinations, services, and distances. Use them early so you avoid last-second lane changes.
imageAlt: Provincial highway route marker for Highway 102
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Information and Direction Signs

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Blasting Activities
keyPoint: Blasting is taking place on or near the road and traffic may be required to stop.
summary: Chapter 3 says work-zone signs warn of temporary conditions and require drivers to slow down and act early. In this case, blasting is taking place on or near the road and traffic may be required to stop.
imageAlt: Orange work-zone warning sign for blasting activity
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Construction / Work Zone Signs

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Bridge Opening
keyPoint: A bridge that can open for boats is ahead.
summary: Chapter 3 explains that warning signs alert drivers to hazards or changing road conditions ahead. In this case, a bridge that can open for boats is ahead.
imageAlt: Yellow warning sign showing an opening bridge
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Warning Signs

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Broken Centre Line With Solid Line
keyPoint: Passing is permitted only when the broken yellow line is on your side and conditions are safe.
summary: Chapter 3 says pavement markings divide lanes, show permitted movements, and warn when passing or lane changes are unsafe. In this case, passing is permitted only when the broken yellow line is on your side and conditions are safe.
imageAlt: Pavement marking diagram with one broken yellow line and one solid yellow line
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Pavement Markings

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Broken Centre Line
keyPoint: Passing with care is permitted in both directions when the centre line is broken.
summary: Chapter 3 says pavement markings divide lanes, show permitted movements, and warn when passing or lane changes are unsafe. In this case, passing with care is permitted in both directions when the centre line is broken.
imageAlt: Pavement marking diagram with a broken yellow centre line
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Pavement Markings

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Bump or Rough Road Ahead
keyPoint: A bump or rough road is ahead; slow down.
summary: Chapter 3 explains that warning signs alert drivers to hazards or changing road conditions ahead. In this case, a bump or rough road is ahead; slow down.
imageAlt: Yellow diamond warning sign for a bump or rough road
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Warning Signs

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Chevron Arrow Signs
keyPoint: Chevron arrows guide drivers around a sharp curve.
summary: Chapter 3 explains that warning signs alert drivers to hazards or changing road conditions ahead. In this case, chevron arrows guide drivers around a sharp curve.
imageAlt: Black and yellow chevron arrow sign
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Warning Signs

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Climbing Lanes
keyPoint: A climbing lane lets faster traffic pass slower vehicles; keep right unless passing.
summary: Chapter 3 says pavement markings divide lanes, show permitted movements, and warn when passing or lane changes are unsafe. In this case, a climbing lane lets faster traffic pass slower vehicles; keep right unless passing.
imageAlt: Pavement diagram showing a climbing lane
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Pavement Markings

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Construction Activities 1 5 Km Ahead
keyPoint: Construction starts 1.5 km ahead; choose another route or prepare for delays.
summary: Chapter 3 says work-zone signs warn of temporary conditions and require drivers to slow down and act early. In this case, construction starts 1.5 km ahead; choose another route or prepare for delays.
imageAlt: Orange work-zone sign showing construction ahead distance
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Construction / Work Zone Signs

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Construction Traffic Entering Right
keyPoint: Construction traffic may enter or leave the road on the right and may be slower than through traffic.
summary: Chapter 3 says work-zone signs warn of temporary conditions and require drivers to slow down and act early. In this case, construction traffic may enter or leave the road on the right and may be slower than through traffic.
imageAlt: Orange work-zone warning sign for construction traffic entering from the right
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Construction / Work Zone Signs

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Continuity Lines
keyPoint: Continuity lines separate through lanes from entry or exit ramps.
summary: Chapter 3 says pavement markings divide lanes, show permitted movements, and warn when passing or lane changes are unsafe. In this case, continuity lines separate through lanes from entry or exit ramps.
imageAlt: Pavement marking diagram with wide continuity lines near a ramp
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Pavement Markings

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Dangerous Road Surface
keyPoint: The road surface may be rutted, grooved, or otherwise hazardous, especially for bicycles or motorcycles.
summary: Chapter 3 says work-zone signs warn of temporary conditions and require drivers to slow down and act early. In this case, the road surface may be rutted, grooved, or otherwise hazardous, especially for bicycles or motorcycles.
imageAlt: Orange sign warning of hazardous road surface conditions
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Construction / Work Zone Signs

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Difference In Elevation Between Lanes
keyPoint: There is a height difference between lanes that could catch a tire and cause loss of control.
summary: Chapter 3 says work-zone signs warn of temporary conditions and require drivers to slow down and act early. In this case, there is a height difference between lanes that could catch a tire and cause loss of control.
imageAlt: Orange work-zone sign warning of uneven lane elevation
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Construction / Work Zone Signs

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Divided Highway Ahead
keyPoint: A divided highway begins ahead.
summary: Chapter 3 explains that warning signs alert drivers to hazards or changing road conditions ahead. In this case, a divided highway begins ahead.
imageAlt: Yellow warning sign showing a divided highway beginning
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Warning Signs

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Divided Highway Ends
keyPoint: A divided highway ends and two-way traffic continues ahead.
summary: Chapter 3 explains that warning signs alert drivers to hazards or changing road conditions ahead. In this case, a divided highway ends and two-way traffic continues ahead.
imageAlt: Yellow warning sign showing a divided highway ending
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Warning Signs

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Do Not Litter
keyPoint: Do not litter from your vehicle or along the roadway.
summary: Chapter 3 says regulatory signs state laws or rules drivers must obey. In this case, do not litter from your vehicle or along the roadway.
imageAlt: Regulatory sign showing a no-littering symbol
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Regulatory Signs

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Do Not Pass
keyPoint: Do not pass other vehicles.
summary: Chapter 3 says regulatory signs state laws or rules drivers must obey. In this case, do not pass other vehicles.
imageAlt: Regulatory sign with a red prohibition symbol for passing
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Regulatory Signs

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Drive to the Right of the Sign
keyPoint: Drive to the right of this sign or obstruction.
summary: Chapter 3 says work-zone signs warn of temporary conditions and require drivers to slow down and act early. In this case, drive to the right of this sign or obstruction.
imageAlt: Regulatory work-zone sign directing traffic to the right
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Construction / Work Zone Signs

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Fire Truck Entrance Ahead
keyPoint: A fire truck entrance is ahead; watch for fire trucks entering or leaving.
summary: Chapter 3 explains that warning signs alert drivers to hazards or changing road conditions ahead. In this case, a fire truck entrance is ahead; watch for fire trucks entering or leaving.
imageAlt: Yellow warning sign showing a fire truck entrance
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Warning Signs

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Flashing Light Unit Caution Mode
keyPoint: A straight bar of lamps is caution mode, warning of a hazard without saying it is safe to pass.
summary: Chapter 3 explains that flashing light units warn of lane closures, obstructions, or active hazards. In this case, a straight bar of lamps is caution mode, warning of a hazard without saying it is safe to pass.
imageAlt: Flashing light unit with lamps arranged in a straight bar
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Flashing Light Units

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Flashing Light Unit Single Arrow
keyPoint: The lane ahead is closed or obstructed; move over and pass only on the side shown by the arrow.
summary: Chapter 3 explains that flashing light units warn of lane closures, obstructions, or active hazards. In this case, the lane ahead is closed or obstructed; move over and pass only on the side shown by the arrow.
imageAlt: Flashing arrow board with a single directional arrow
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Flashing Light Units

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Green Circle Permission
keyPoint: A green circle gives permission for the movement or action shown.
summary: Chapter 3 says regulatory signs state laws or rules drivers must obey. In this case, a green circle gives permission for the movement or action shown.
imageAlt: Regulatory sign with a green circle symbol
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Regulatory Signs

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Hazard Warning Drive Left
keyPoint: Hazard marker lines slope left; drive to the left of the sign.
summary: Chapter 3 explains that warning signs alert drivers to hazards or changing road conditions ahead. In this case, hazard marker lines slope left; drive to the left of the sign.
imageAlt: Black and yellow hazard marker with lines sloping left
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Warning Signs

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Hazard Warning Drive Right
keyPoint: Hazard marker lines slope right; drive to the right of the sign.
summary: Chapter 3 explains that warning signs alert drivers to hazards or changing road conditions ahead. In this case, hazard marker lines slope right; drive to the right of the sign.
imageAlt: Black and yellow hazard marker with lines sloping right
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Warning Signs

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Hidden Intersection Ahead
keyPoint: A hidden intersection is ahead; side-road drivers may not clearly see approaching traffic.
summary: Chapter 3 explains that warning signs alert drivers to hazards or changing road conditions ahead. In this case, a hidden intersection is ahead; side-road drivers may not clearly see approaching traffic.
imageAlt: Yellow warning sign for a hidden intersection
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Warning Signs

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Keep to the Right
keyPoint: Keep to the right of the traffic island or obstruction.
summary: Chapter 3 says regulatory signs state laws or rules drivers must obey. In this case, keep to the right of the traffic island or obstruction.
imageAlt: Regulatory sign directing drivers to keep right
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Regulatory Signs

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Left Edge Solid Yellow Line Divided Highway
keyPoint: A solid yellow line marks the left edge of a divided highway.
summary: Chapter 3 says pavement markings divide lanes, show permitted movements, and warn when passing or lane changes are unsafe. In this case, a solid yellow line marks the left edge of a divided highway; seeing it on the wrong side may mean you are travelling the wrong way.
imageAlt: Pavement marking diagram with a left-edge solid yellow line
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Pavement Markings

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Low Clearance
keyPoint: A low-clearance underpass is ahead; check that your vehicle can pass under it.
summary: Chapter 3 explains that warning signs alert drivers to hazards or changing road conditions ahead. In this case, a low-clearance underpass is ahead; check that your vehicle can pass under it.
imageAlt: Yellow low-clearance warning sign
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Warning Signs

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Maximum Speed Limit
keyPoint: This is the maximum speed allowed under ideal conditions.
summary: Chapter 3 says regulatory signs state laws or rules drivers must obey. In this case, this is the maximum speed allowed under ideal conditions.
imageAlt: White rectangular maximum speed limit sign
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Regulatory Signs

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Narrow Structure Ahead
keyPoint: A narrow road, bridge, culvert, underpass, or similar structure is ahead.
summary: Chapter 3 explains that warning signs alert drivers to hazards or changing road conditions ahead. In this case, a narrow road, bridge, culvert, underpass, or similar structure is ahead.
imageAlt: Yellow warning sign for a narrow structure ahead
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Warning Signs

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: No Left Turn
keyPoint: No left turn is permitted.
summary: Chapter 3 says regulatory signs state laws or rules drivers must obey. In this case, no left turn is permitted.
imageAlt: No-left-turn regulatory sign with a red slash
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Regulatory Signs

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: No Parking Either Direction
keyPoint: No parking is allowed in either direction from this sign.
summary: Chapter 3 says regulatory signs state laws or rules drivers must obey. In this case, no parking is allowed in either direction from this sign.
imageAlt: No-parking sign with arrows in both directions
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Regulatory Signs

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: No Right Turn on Red
keyPoint: Do not turn right on a red signal; wait for a green light.
summary: Chapter 3 says regulatory signs state laws or rules drivers must obey. In this case, do not turn right on a red signal; wait for a green light.
imageAlt: No-right-turn-on-red regulatory sign
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Regulatory Signs

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: No Stopping
keyPoint: No stopping is allowed.
summary: Chapter 3 says regulatory signs state laws or rules drivers must obey. In this case, no stopping is allowed.
imageAlt: No-stopping regulatory sign
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Regulatory Signs

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: No Turns
keyPoint: No turns are permitted.

keyPoint: Do not pass; the sign creates an orderly flow before a merge or where passing is unsafe.

imageAlt: Orange work-zone regulatory sign indicating no passing

chapter: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road
title: Left-Turn Wheels
keyPoint: Straight ahead until you can complete the turn.
summary: Keep your wheels straight while waiting to turn left. If hit from behind, turned wheels can push you into oncoming traffic.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Right-of-Way at Intersections

chapter: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road
title: Passing Cyclists
keyPoint: Leave plenty of space and pass only when safe.
summary: Cyclists are vulnerable road users. Give space, check for oncoming traffic, and pass only when it can be done safely.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Sharing the Road with Bicycles

chapter: Chapter 5 — Adverse Driving Conditions
title: Truck Blind Spots
keyPoint: Stay out of blind spots and leave extra room.
summary: Large vehicles have bigger blind spots and need more space to stop and turn. Keep visible and avoid cutting in.
handbookSection: Chapter 5 — Adverse Driving Conditions, Trucker's Blind Spots

chapter: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road
title: Slow-Moving Equipment
keyPoint: Slow down and pass only when legal and safe.
summary: Slow-moving vehicles may turn wide or move unpredictably. Reduce speed and pass only when you have clear sight distance and it is legal.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Encountering Other Road Users

chapter: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road
title: Unsafe Posted Speed
keyPoint: Drive slower than the posted limit.
summary: The posted limit is a maximum for ideal conditions. Slow down when weather, visibility, traffic, or road surface conditions require it.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Speed Limits

chapter: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road
title: Two-Second Rule
keyPoint: At least two seconds behind the vehicle ahead.
summary: Use at least a two-second following distance in good conditions. Increase the space when conditions are poor.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Two-Second Rule

chapter: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road
title: Extra Following Distance
keyPoint: In rain, snow, darkness, heavy traffic, or when following large vehicles.
summary: Poor visibility, slippery roads, heavy traffic, and large vehicles all increase stopping needs. More space gives you time to react.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Two-Second Rule

chapter: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road
title: Dooring Check
keyPoint: Check for vehicles and cyclists.
summary: Check mirrors and shoulder before opening a door. Opening into a cyclist or vehicle path can cause a serious collision.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Parking and Stopping

chapter: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road
title: Downhill Parking
keyPoint: Toward the curb or roadside.
summary: Turn wheels toward the curb when parking downhill. This helps the curb stop the vehicle if it rolls.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Parking on a Hill

chapter: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road
title: Passing Sight Distance
keyPoint: When you cannot see far enough ahead.
summary: Do not pass unless you have enough clear sight distance and the law allows it. If in doubt, wait.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Overtaking and Passing

chapter: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road
title: Lane Change Checks
keyPoint: Signal, check mirrors, check blind spot, then move when safe.
summary: A safe lane change includes signalling, mirror checks, a shoulder check, and moving only when the space is clear.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Signalling and Lane Changes

chapter: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road
title: Crosswalk Passing
keyPoint: Do not pass the stopped vehicle.
summary: Never pass a vehicle stopped at a crosswalk for a pedestrian. A pedestrian may be hidden from your view.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Overtaking and Passing

chapter: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road
title: Highway Merging
keyPoint: Match traffic speed, signal, and merge when safe.
summary: Use the acceleration lane to build speed, signal, and merge into a safe gap. Avoid stopping unless traffic conditions require it.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Controlled-Access Highways

chapter: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road
title: Missed Highway Exit
keyPoint: Continue to the next exit.
summary: Never back up or make sudden illegal moves on a highway. Continue to the next exit and return safely.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Controlled-Access Highways

chapter: Chapter 4 — Safety
title: Escape Space
keyPoint: It can block escape space and keep you in a blind spot.
summary: Keeping space around your vehicle gives you options if something changes. Avoid lingering in another driver’s blind spot.
handbookSection: Chapter 4 — Safety, Defensive Driving and Blind Spots
